text:
DEPARTMENT OF PATHOLOGY AND LABORATORY MEDICINE
SURGICAL PATHOLOGY REPORT

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PATIENT: Ellison, Marcus R.
MRN: [REDACTED]
ACCESSION NUMBER: SP-[REDACTED]
ATTENDING ONCOLOGIST: [REDACTED]
SUBMITTING SERVICE: Medical Oncology
CLINICAL INDICATION: 56-year-old male with known Stage IV (M1c) BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic melanoma, primary site skin of back; presenting with radiographic progressive disease after 8 months of dabrafenib/trametinib (BRAF/MEK inhibitor) combination therapy. Biopsy of new subcutaneous nodule on right abdomen for confirmation of melanoma and assessment of acquired resistance mechanisms. Prior pathology on file (SP-[REDACTED]; right axillary lymph node core biopsy and primary excision, [prior accession]).
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

SPECIMEN SUBMITTED:
  Specimen A: "Right abdominal subcutaneous nodule, core needle biopsy" — Three (3) cores of tan-gray soft tissue, each approximately 1.3–1.5 cm in length and 1.5 mm in diameter, received in formalin. Total submitted: three cores, entirely processed.

DATE OF PROCEDURE: [DATE REDACTED — TO BE INSERTED PROGRAMMATICALLY]
PROCEDURE TYPE: CT-guided core needle biopsy, 16-gauge Bard Max-Core needle, three passes. Procedure performed by Interventional Radiology.
FIXATIVE: 10% neutral buffered formalin
CLINICAL HISTORY: Stage IV metastatic melanoma, progressive disease on dabrafenib/trametinib. New right abdominal subcutaneous nodule identified on restaging CT (1.7 cm). Biopsy obtained for resistance profiling and re-confirmation of diagnosis.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DIAGNOSIS:

A. RIGHT ABDOMINAL SUBCUTANEOUS NODULE, CORE NEEDLE BIOPSY:
   METASTATIC MELANOMA
   — See comment.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
GROSS DESCRIPTION:

Specimen A is received in a labeled formalin-fixed container designated "Right abdominal subcutaneous nodule, core needle biopsy." The container holds three tan-gray, cylindrical soft tissue cores measuring 1.3 cm x 0.15 cm, 1.5 cm x 0.15 cm, and 1.4 cm x 0.15 cm, respectively. The tissue is firm and homogeneous in cut section, without grossly visible necrosis or hemorrhage on the individual cores, though one core demonstrates a faint gray-tan discoloration at its center. All three cores are submitted in their entirety in one cassette (A1), embedded in standard orientation.
Representative sections submitted in one cassette: A1 (all three cores).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION:

Sections show fibroadipose tissue with a cellular neoplastic infiltrate occupying approximately 80–90% of the core material. The tumor is composed of sheets and irregular nests of epithelioid to spindled cells with markedly enlarged, pleomorphic nuclei, prominent eosinophilic nucleoli (often macronucleoli), and moderate to abundant pale eosinophilic cytoplasm. Occasional binucleated forms are identified. Intracytoplasmic melanin pigment is focally present in scattered tumor cells. The mitotic rate is brisk, with numerous conventional and atypical mitotic figures readily identified; mitotic count estimated at greater than 10 per mm2 in the most active foci. Areas of geographic necrosis are present, comprising approximately 15–20% of the viable tumor volume. Thin fibrovascular septae traverse the tumor, with scattered lymphocytic infiltration at the tumor-stroma interface, though overall the tumor-infiltrating lymphocyte (TIL) pattern appears regressed compared to expected for treatment-naive melanoma (classified as non-brisk/regressed TILs). Stromal sclerosis and hyalinization are focally present at the periphery of tumor nests, consistent with treatment-related stromal changes in the setting of prior targeted therapy. No normal subcutaneous adnexal structures are identified within the biopsy cores, consistent with complete effacement by tumor. No perineural invasion is identified in the sampled material. Lymphovascular invasion cannot be definitively assessed on core biopsy material.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
ANCILLARY STUDIES:

IMMUNOHISTOCHEMISTRY (performed at [REDACTED] Laboratory, accession [REDACTED]):
The following immunohistochemical stains were performed on block A1 with appropriate positive and negative controls:

  S100 protein:         POSITIVE — diffuse, strong cytoplasmic and nuclear staining in tumor cells
  HMB-45 (gp100):      POSITIVE — diffuse, strong cytoplasmic granular staining in tumor cells
  Melan-A (MART-1):    POSITIVE — diffuse cytoplasmic staining in tumor cells
  SOX10:               POSITIVE — diffuse nuclear staining in tumor cells
  Ki-67 (MIB-1):       POSITIVE — proliferative index approximately 60–65% of tumor nuclei
  PAN-CK (AE1/AE3):   NEGATIVE — confirms non-epithelial lineage
  CD45 (LCA):          NEGATIVE — excludes lymphomatous involvement

  BRAF V600E (VE1 clone, Spring Bioscience):
                        POSITIVE — strong, diffuse cytoplasmic staining in tumor cells (3+ intensity)
                        [Note: VE1 immunoreactivity is retained in the setting of acquired BRAF amplification-mediated resistance; molecular testing remains necessary to characterize resistance mechanism.]

  PD-L1 (28-8 PharmDx assay, Dako/Agilent):
                        RESULT PENDING — sample submitted to reference laboratory; result expected within 5–7 business days. [Addendum to follow.]

MOLECULAR / NEXT-GENERATION SEQUENCING:
  Tissue has been submitted for comprehensive somatic tumor molecular profiling (523-gene NGS panel) to assess for acquired resistance mechanisms to BRAF/MEK inhibitor therapy. Result pending — addendum report to follow.
  [Institutional molecular pathology laboratory case number: MP-[REDACTED]]

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
COMMENT:

The morphologic and immunophenotypic features of this right abdominal subcutaneous core needle biopsy are consistent with metastatic melanoma, concordant with the patient's known diagnosis of BRAF V600E-mutant Stage IV melanoma, primary site skin of back. The current biopsy shows high-grade histologic features including brisk mitotic activity (estimated >10/mm2), areas of geographic necrosis, and a markedly elevated Ki-67 proliferative index of approximately 60–65%, representing a notable increase in proliferative activity compared to the diagnostic specimen obtained at initial staging (right axillary lymph node core biopsy; Ki-67 approximately 40% at that time). This increase in mitotic rate and proliferative index is consistent with clonal evolution and biologic acceleration in the setting of acquired resistance to BRAF/MEK inhibitor therapy, as has been well-described in the literature.

Regressed/non-brisk tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes and treatment-associated stromal fibrosis are present, consistent with prior exposure to targeted therapy. Retained VE1 (BRAF V600E) immunoreactivity indicates that the BRAF V600E oncoprotein is preserved in the resistant clone; the resistance mechanism is therefore not attributable to loss of the target mutation, and molecular profiling (result pending) is necessary to characterize the specific resistance mechanism (e.g., BRAF amplification, BRAF splice variant, MEK1/2 mutation, NRAS mutation, or alternative pathway activation).

This material has been forwarded for somatic NGS molecular profiling as noted above. Correlation with clinical history, prior molecular data, and pending molecular results is recommended.

Comparison with prior material: Prior right axillary lymph node core biopsy (SP-[REDACTED]) demonstrated metastatic melanoma with Ki-67 ~40%, mitotic rate 6/mm2, S100+/HMB-45+/Melan-A+/SOX10+/BRAF VE1+, and PD-L1 TPS 25%/CPS 38. The current biopsy is morphologically consistent with the prior diagnosis, with evidence of histologic progression.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DIAGNOSING PATHOLOGIST: [REDACTED], M.D.
Department of Pathology and Laboratory Medicine

ATTENDING SIGN-OUT PATHOLOGIST: [REDACTED], M.D., Ph.D.
Director, Dermatopathology and Surgical Pathology
Department of Pathology and Laboratory Medicine

REPORT STATUS: FINAL (Addendum pending for PD-L1 result and NGS result)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
ADDENDUM [PENDING — TO BE ISSUED UPON RECEIPT OF PD-L1 AND NGS RESULTS]
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Electronic signature applied by [REDACTED], M.D., Ph.D.
Case reviewed and signed out on [DATE REDACTED — TO BE INSERTED PROGRAMMATICALLY]

--- END OF REPORT ---
